steps.prompt: Which word is the main verb?

hints.tandem: 'tandem' is an adverb meaning 'at last / finally'

hints.to take away: 'to take away' is 'auferre' — different verb entirely
hints.to be absent: 'to be absent' is 'abesse' — don't confuse the ab- prefix

hints.Present indicative: Present of deponent 'abutor' would be 'abuteris' — 'abutere' is the future form
hints.Present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'abutaris' — this is a future indicative form
hints.Imperfect subjunctive: Imperfect subjunctive would be 'abuterēris' — 'abutere' is future indicative

hints.utor, uti, usus sum: These are the parts of 'uti' (to use) — missing the ab- prefix
hints.abiicio, abiicere, abieci, abiectum: These are the parts of 'abiicere' (to throw away) — a different ab- compound
hints.abeo, abire, abii, abitum: These are the parts of 'abire' (to go away) — a different verb

hints.Nominative: Nominative would make Catiline the subject, but Cicero is addressing him directly
hints.Ablative: Ablative would require a preposition or different context — Cicero is calling out to Catiline
hints.Accusative: Accusative would be 'Catilinam' — this is the vocative of direct address

hints.2nd: 2nd declension masculine nouns end in -us — 'Catilina' is a 1st declension masculine noun
hints.3rd: 3rd declension nouns have varied nominative endings — 'Catilina' ends in -a (1st declension)
hints.4th: 4th declension nouns end in -us/-u — 'Catilina' is 1st declension

hints.Nominative: Nominative 'patientia' is the same form, but 'abuti' governs the ablative case
hints.Accusative: Accusative would be 'patientiam' — 'abuti' takes the ablative
hints.Vocative: Cicero is not addressing patience — 'patientia' is the object of 'abuti'

hints.2nd: 2nd declension nouns end in -us/-um — 'patientia' with -ia ending is 1st declension
hints.3rd: 3rd declension nouns have varied endings — 'patientia' is a typical 1st declension -a noun
hints.5th: 5th declension nouns end in -es — 'patientia' is 1st declension

steps.prompt: What rhetorical effect does this opening question achieve?

hints.A polite request for information: Cicero is not seeking information — this is a rhetorical question expressing outrage
hints.A calm philosophical inquiry: The words 'tandem' (finally!) and 'abutere' (abuse) convey anger, not calm inquiry
hints.An expression of fear: Cicero projects authority and indignation here, not fear

translation: How far, at last, will you abuse our patience, Catiline?
